questions.explanation: Any positive number is greater than any negative number, so 4 > -3. A common mistake is focusing on the minus sign as if it makes the whole comparison larger.
Among negative numbers, the number closer to zero is greater. Since -2 is closer to zero than -9, -2 > -9. A common mistake is thinking the larger digit makes the number smaller.
Zero is greater than any negative number, so 0 > -1. A common mistake is forgetting that zero sits to the right of all negative numbers on the number line.
-1 is greater than -6 because it is closer to zero. A common mistake is choosing -6 because 6 is a larger digit.

questions.questionText: Choose the correct comparison for 7 and 0.
questions.explanation: Any positive number is greater than zero, so 7 > 0. A common mistake is thinking zero is automatically greater because it has no sign.
-10 is farther left on the number line than -3, so -10 < -3. A common mistake is thinking 10 being a larger digit makes -10 greater.
A warmer temperature is represented by the greater number. Since 2 > -4, 2 degrees is warmer. A common mistake is thinking negative numbers are warmer because the digit looks larger.

Among negative numbers, the one closer to zero is greater. Since -5 is closer to zero than -8, -5 > -8. A common mistake is comparing the digits without thinking about their locations on the number line.
Any negative number is less than any positive number, so -12 < 5. A common mistake is seeing 12 as a large digit and forgetting the negative sign.
Negative numbers are less than zero and positive numbers. That makes -2 the smallest number in the set. A common mistake is picking 0 because it looks neutral.

questions.questionText: A student says -9 > -4 because 9 is greater than 4. What is the mistake?
options: The student forgot that farther left means smaller
The student should always choose the larger digit
questions.correctAnswer: The student forgot that farther left means smaller
questions.explanation: On the number line, -9 is farther left than -4, so it is smaller, not greater. A common mistake is comparing the digits and forgetting how negative numbers work.

questions.questionText: Which number belongs in the blank: -3 ___ -3?
questions.explanation: A number is always equal to itself, so -3 = -3. A common mistake is thinking the negative sign changes one side more than the other.
-1 is to the left of 0 on the number line, so -1 < 0. A common mistake is thinking zero is less because it has no sign.

Among negative numbers, the value closer to zero is greater. Since -11 is closer to zero than -15 or -20, it is the greatest of the three. A common mistake is choosing -20 because 20 is the largest digit.

questions.questionText: An elevator is at floor -2 and then moves to floor 3. Which floor is greater?
questions.explanation: Positive floors are greater than negative floors, so floor 3 is greater than floor -2. A common mistake is thinking underground floors count as larger because the digit 2 is smaller than 3.

questions.questionText: Which number line idea correctly compares -7 and -2?
options: They are at the same point
Both are to the right of zero
questions.explanation: Numbers farther to the right are greater. Since -2 is to the right of -7, -2 > -7. A common mistake is thinking -7 is greater because 7 is a larger digit.
Any positive number is greater than any negative number, so 12 > -6. A common mistake is focusing on the digit 6 and ignoring the sign.
-13 is farther left on the number line than -5, so it is smaller. A common mistake is thinking 13 being a larger digit makes -13 greater.
The smallest number is farthest left on the number line. Among these choices, -9 is farthest left. A common mistake is choosing 0 because it looks central.
Among negative numbers, the value closer to zero is greater. Since -2 is closest to zero, it is the greatest. A common mistake is choosing -11 because 11 is the largest digit.
Zero is less than any positive number, so 0 < 7. A common mistake is thinking zero should be greater because it has no sign.

questions.questionText: Which symbol belongs in the blank: -20 ___ -20?
questions.explanation: A number is always equal to itself, so -20 = -20. A common mistake is thinking repeated negative signs change the comparison.
The colder temperature is the smaller number. Since -7 is less than -1 and 3, -7 degrees is colder. A common mistake is choosing -1 because 1 is smaller than 7.
Any positive number is greater than zero and any negative number, so 3 is greater than -100. A common mistake is thinking 100 being a larger digit makes -100 greater.

questions.questionText: A student says 0 < -6 because 0 is a small number. What is the mistake?
options: Zero is to the right of negative numbers
questions.correctAnswer: Zero is to the right of negative numbers
questions.explanation: On the number line, zero is to the right of every negative number, so 0 > -6. A common mistake is using the word small instead of number-line position.

questions.questionText: Which symbol makes the statement true: 5 ___ -5?
questions.explanation: A positive number is greater than a negative number, so 5 > -5. A common mistake is thinking the numbers are equal because they use the same digit.

questions.questionText: Which statement about -30 and -12 is true?
options: -12 is greater because it is closer to zero
-30 is greater because 30 is larger than 12
questions.correctAnswer: -12 is greater because it is closer to zero
questions.explanation: Among negative numbers, the one closer to zero is greater. Since -12 is closer to zero than -30, -12 > -30. A common mistake is focusing only on the digits.
The lowest elevation is represented by the smallest number. Since -8 is less than -3, 1, and 4, it is the lowest. A common mistake is choosing -3 because 3 is smaller than 8.
Any negative number is less than any positive number, so -2 < 2. A common mistake is choosing -2 > 2 because the digit 2 appears on both sides.
Zero is greater than any negative number. That makes 0 the greatest value in the set. A common mistake is choosing -1 because it is closest to zero without noticing zero is included.

options: -14 is farther left on the number line
questions.correctAnswer: -14 is farther left on the number line
questions.explanation: Numbers farther left on the number line are smaller. Since -14 is left of -9, it is less. A common mistake is looking only at the absolute values 14 and 9.
-50 is farther left on the number line than -45, so it is smaller. A common mistake is choosing -50 because 50 is the larger digit.

questions.questionText: Which number line idea correctly compares 6 and -6?
options: They are at the same point
questions.explanation: Positive numbers are to the right of negative numbers. Since 6 is to the right of -6, 6 > -6. A common mistake is thinking matching digits mean matching positions.

questions.questionText: Which symbol belongs in the blank: 0 ___ 0?
questions.explanation: A number is always equal to itself, so 0 = 0. A common mistake is looking for a greater-or-less comparison when the numbers match exactly.
Among negative balances, the one closer to zero is greater. Since -7 is closer to zero than -12, -7 dollars is greater. A common mistake is choosing -12 because 12 is the larger digit.
The warmest temperature is represented by the greatest number. Since 3 is greater than 0, -2, and -5, it is warmest. A common mistake is focusing only on whether a temperature is below zero.
-1 is closer to zero than -10, so it is greater. A common mistake is choosing -10 because 10 is a larger digit.

options: Positive numbers are to the right of zero
questions.correctAnswer: Positive numbers are to the right of zero
questions.explanation: On the number line, positive numbers lie to the right of zero, so 8 > 0. A common mistake is thinking zero should automatically be greater than any other number.
-100 is farther left than -99 on the number line, so it is smaller. A common mistake is choosing -100 because 100 is the larger digit.

questions.questionText: Which number is neither positive nor negative?
questions.explanation: Zero is neither positive nor negative. A common mistake is treating zero as positive because it is not negative.
A number is equal to itself, so 11 = 11. A common mistake is assuming one side must be greater because the problem asks for a comparison.

questions.questionText: A student says -4 < -9 because 4 is less than 9. What is the mistake?
options: -4 is actually greater because it is closer to zero
questions.correctAnswer: -4 is actually greater because it is closer to zero
questions.explanation: Among negative numbers, the value closer to zero is greater. Since -4 is closer to zero than -9, -4 > -9. A common mistake is comparing only the digits 4 and 9.

The greatest elevation is represented by the largest number. Since 2 is greater than 0, -1, and -6, 2 feet is greatest. A common mistake is choosing 0 because it separates positive and negative values.
A number is equal to itself, so -6 = -6. A common mistake is thinking two negatives must produce a less-than statement.
The smallest number is farthest left on the number line. Among these values, -30 is farthest left. A common mistake is choosing -3 because 3 is the smaller digit.

questions.questionText: Which number line idea correctly compares -15 and -1?
options: They are at the same point
Both are to the right of zero
questions.explanation: Numbers farther right are greater. Since -1 is to the right of -15, -1 > -15. A common mistake is thinking -15 is greater because 15 is a larger digit.
Any negative number is less than any positive number, so -25 < 5. A common mistake is focusing on the size of 25 instead of the negative sign.

questions.questionText: Which statement about zero is true?
options: Zero is greater than every negative number
Zero is less than every negative number
questions.correctAnswer: Zero is greater than every negative number
questions.explanation: Zero is to the right of every negative number on the number line, so it is greater than all of them. A common mistake is treating zero as if it were negative because it is not positive.
Any positive number is greater than any negative number, so 14 > -14. A common mistake is thinking opposite-looking numbers should be equal in size and order.
Among negative numbers, the value closer to zero is greater. Since -150 is closer to zero than -200, it is greater. A common mistake is choosing -200 because 200 is the larger digit.
